researchFindings.claim: Know your evacuation zone before fire season — many counties use a letter or number system; find your zone at your county's emergency management website and know what each level means
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core preparedness tip — know your zone

researchFindings.claim: The Ready-Set-Go model: READY = prepare year-round; SET = when a wildfire threat is in your area, be ready to go immediately; GO = leave as soon as an evacuation warning or order is issued — do not wait for an order if you feel unsafe
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Go Evacuation Guide | Ready for Wildfire
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core preparedness framework — Ready Set Go model

researchFindings.claim: Prepack a go-bag with at least 72 hours of supplies: water, non-perishable food, N95 or P100 respirator mask for smoke, current medications, phone charger, cash, copies of critical documents, a change of clothing, battery-powered radio, and a flashlight
Maintain at least half a tank of gas in your vehicle at all times during fire season — gas stations may be closed or gridlocked during an evacuation

Create defensible space: Zone 1 (0–30 feet from structures) — remove all dead vegetation, flammable debris, and woodpiles; Zone 2 (30–100 feet) — space plants so they cannot carry fire from plant to plant; minimum spacing between plants is 3 times the plant's diameter
Ensure there are at least two ways out of your neighborhood; practice both evacuation routes by driving them so you know them when conditions are poor
Sign up for emergency alerts through your county — most counties use Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) automatically and a local alert system (Nixle, Everbridge, or county-specific) requiring sign-up
Keep cellphones charged; set phones to auto-charge overnight during fire season
Early evacuation dramatically improves survival odds in fast-moving wildfires — residents who leave early spend less time in smoke and face lighter traffic than those who wait for a mandatory order
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Go Evacuation Guide | Ready for Wildfire
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Evacuation timing warning — strongest preparedness message

researchFindings.claim: Pre-select a family meeting place and designate an out-of-area contact in case family members are separated during evacuation
Do not shelter in your home if fire reaches your structure — a burning structure is more dangerous than open air; however, if evacuation routes are cut off, go inside and call 911
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Critical during-fire guidance, referenced in warnings

conflictsOrWeakClaims.claim: Whether to shelter inside vs. leave when fire is at the doorstep
conflictsOrWeakClaims.issue: Some sources (FEMA) say if routes are cut off go inside and call 911; CAL FIRE generally says leave early. These are not truly contradictory — early departure is preferred, but last-resort indoor shelter is a fallback.
conflictsOrWeakClaims.resolution: Include both: preferred action = leave early; backup = if routes are cut off, shelter inside and call 911. Clearly label as last resort.

identifiedGaps.area: Specific hardening materials (ember-resistant vents, fire-resistant roofing)
identifiedGaps.impact: Important for homeowners in WUI zones but too technical for a general preparedness guide
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Mention home hardening briefly and direct to USFA for detailed guidance

recommendation: Write a preparedness_guide for the Wildfire: Evacuation and Smoke Safety parentTopic. Core content: (1) know your evacuation zone and routes; (2) Ready-Set-Go framework; (3) go-bag contents including N95 mask; (4) keep gas tank half-full; (5) defensible space zones 0–30 and 30–100 feet; (6) sign up for local emergency alerts; (7) family plan with meeting place and out-of-area contact. Key message: leave early, don't wait for mandatory order. All claims sourced by FEMA, USFA, and CAL FIRE.
